title: Dog Ate Avocado Skin and Pit: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate an avocado pit or skin, learn about the risks of blockage, persin toxicity, and when to contact a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog eats an avocado pit or skin, the primary danger is a life-threatening physical blockage in the digestive tract. Seek immediate emergency veterinary care if your dog is choking, gasping, or vomiting repeatedly.

What are the health risks if my dog eats avocado skin or the pit?

  • If your dog has consumed avocado skin or the pit, there are two primary concerns: persin toxicity and physical obstruction.
  • Persin is a fungicidal toxin found in avocados; while dogs are more resistant to it than other animals, the skin and pit contain the highest concentrations and can cause significant stomach upset or irritation.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog swallowed an avocado pit?

  • The urgency level for this situation is Medium, as the greatest danger is typically the pit acting as a foreign body rather than the toxin itself.
  • Because the pit is large, hard, and slippery, it can easily become lodged in the esophagus, stomach, or intestines.
  • If your dog is currently choking, gasping for air, or vomiting repeatedly, please proceed to an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How can providing a photo help the vet triage my dog?

  • Providing a photo of the avocado variety or the remaining parts of the fruit is extremely helpful for the veterinary team or pet poison helpline.
  • Seeing the size and shape of the pit allows the team to estimate the likelihood of it passing through your dog's digestive tract based on your pet's size and weight.
  • A photo of any vomited material can also help determine if the pit has been partially or fully expelled from the body.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Although dogs appear relatively resistant to avocado toxicosis compared to other species, ingestion of the fruit, leaves, stems, and seeds of avocado, particularly Guatemalan varieties, has been associated with toxicosis in animals. The toxic principle, persin, can cause myocardial necrosis at high dosages. Clinical findings in other animals include sterile mastitis within 24 hours of exposure, accompanied by a significant decrease in milk production, firm, swollen mammary glands, and watery, curdled milk. Pulmonary edema and free fluid within the abdominal cavity, pericardial sac, and thoracic cavity may also occur. Diagnosis relies on a history of exposure and clinical signs, as there are no readily available specific tests.

Chapter: Toxicology, Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2965)

faqs.question: Can a dog pass an avocado pit naturally?
faqs.answer: While it is possible for very large dogs to pass a pit, it is highly risky. The pit can become stuck at any point in the digestive tract, leading to a life-threatening blockage that may require surgery.

faqs.question: What symptoms of a blockage should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for signs such as persistent vomiting, inability to keep food or water down, lethargy, abdominal pain, or straining to have a bowel movement.

faqs.question: Is the skin of the avocado dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes, the skin is difficult to digest and contains higher levels of persin than the flesh. It can cause gastrointestinal upset, including vomiting and diarrhea.
